name: AWS Amplify
what_it_does: AWS managed backend-as-a-service platform for mobile and web apps, providing authentication (Cognito), GraphQL/REST APIs (AppSync/API Gateway), storage (S3), and hosting with a unified SDK and CLI.
use_cases: Building mobile app backends with authentication, API, and storage without managing servers
Rapid prototyping of full-stack applications using Amplify CLI to generate backend resources
Deploying React/Next.js apps with automatic CI/CD and hosting via Amplify Console
Building offline-capable mobile apps with Amplify DataStore for local-first sync
Multi-tenant SaaS applications using Cognito user pools and AppSync for API
not_for: Teams not using AWS — Amplify is tightly coupled to AWS services
Fine-grained infrastructure control — Amplify abstracts away AWS complexity at the cost of flexibility
High-performance APIs requiring sub-10ms latency at global scale
Applications with complex multi-service architectures beyond what Amplify's opinionated structure supports
best_when: You're building a mobile or web app on AWS and want to get a full-featured backend (auth, API, storage, hosting) up quickly with minimal infrastructure management.
avoid_when: You need full control over your AWS infrastructure, you use a non-AWS cloud, or your backend requirements are simple enough for a lighter solution (Supabase, Firebase).

auth.notes: Multiple auth modes via AppSync: Cognito User Pool, IAM, API key, OpenID Connect, Lambda. Amplify Authenticator UI component handles Cognito auth flows. Auth is per-API and configurable per-field in GraphQL schema.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier: 1,000 build minutes/month, 5GB hosting, AppSync/Cognito/S3 free tiers apply
pricing.paid_tiers: Amplify Hosting: $0.01/build minute, $0.15/GB served; backend services billed separately via AWS
pricing.notes: Amplify adds hosting and CI/CD costs on top of underlying AWS service costs (AppSync, Cognito, S3, Lambda). Costs can be complex to predict across multiple services.

performance.rate_limits: AppSync: 1,000 concurrent connections; Cognito: 120 TPS per user pool

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: AWS error messages are generally functional but can be verbose and technical. AppSync GraphQL errors are well-structured. Cognito auth errors require careful parsing for user-facing messages.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: AppSync mutations are not idempotent by default. Conditional expressions can add idempotency. Amplify DataStore has conflict resolution built in.
known_agent_gotchas: Amplify Gen 1 and Gen 2 have significantly different architecture and APIs — specify which version you're using
AppSync GraphQL resolvers use VTL (Velocity Template Language) by default — steep learning curve
Auth configuration is per-API, not per-app — multi-API apps need consistent auth strategy
Amplify CLI adds cloud resources that persist even after project deletion — manual cleanup required
DataStore offline sync can create conflicts in multi-user scenarios — understand conflict resolution
Build times can be slow (5-15 minutes) — not suitable for tight CI/CD feedback loops
Amplify Console environment variables are separate from Amplify backend parameters — easy to confuse

security_score_components.security_notes: AWS Amplify uses IAM credentials + Cognito for app auth. Admin IAM access controls deployments. Cognito user pools for app users. Amplify environment variables for secrets.
